blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: How long have you known Marcus?

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Twenty-three years. We met freshman year of college.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Mine. I mean — I suggested the house. The group's done trips before, it's something we try to do once a year. This one was my idea.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: When did you last see Marcus alive?

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: On the beach. Around four. He wasn't — he seemed off. Not sick, just quiet. In his head. I went over to check on him and he said he was going to rest before dinner.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: Did he seem like himself otherwise? This weekend?

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Mostly. Breakfast this morning he was great — laughing, making everyone food. But there were moments. Little moments where he'd just go somewhere inside himself.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: Any idea what might have been on his mind?

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: I asked. He said nothing. That was Marcus — he'd carry things.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: Tell me about the argument. Through the ceiling.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Around nine-fifteen, nine-twenty — we heard him upstairs. Voices. His voice and another one. It was heated. We assumed it was a phone call.

blocks.speaker: Davenport
blocks.content: The second voice — could you make out anything? Anything at all?

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Just the tone. Muffled. It was — it sounded like someone angry. Or scared. I couldn't tell which.

blocks.speaker: Cade
blocks.content: Maybe twenty, twenty-five minutes. It stopped around nine forty-five. We figured it was over. We finished the dishes. And then when he still hadn't come down by ten-thirty I went up.
